responses.prompt: Write a 300+ word summary of the wikipedia page "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raymond_III,_Count_of_Tripoli". Do not use any commas and highlight at least 3 sections that has titles in markdown format, for example *highlighted section part 1*, *highlighted section part 2*, *highlighted section part 3*.
responses.text:
We need to write a 300+ word summary of the Wikipedia page "Raymond III, Count of Tripoli". No commas allowed. Need to highlight at least 3 sections that have titles in markdown format, for example *highlighted section part 1*, *highlighted section part 2*, *highlighted section part 3*. I must avoid commas entirely, so I'll need to write without any commas. I can use periods, semicolons, dashes, etc., but no commas. The summary should be over 300 words. I'll read the Wikipedia page. Raymond III, Count of Tripoli (1140–1187) was a Crusader count. I'll summarize main points. I need at least 3 sections with titles in markdown. I'll create my own sections, not necessarily the Wikipedia's sections, but I'll highlight at least 3 parts of my summary as if they had titles. The instruction: "highlight at least 3 sections that has titles in markdown format, for example *highlighted section part 1*..." So I'll structure my summary into multiple paragraphs and perhaps use markdown headings with asterisks and text? The example: *highlighted section part 1* - it's italicized with asterisks, that's markdown for italic, but they said "highlighted section that has titles in markdown format". Possibly they mean using markdown headings like ## Section Title? But the example uses *highlighted section part 1* which is italic text. Could be that I'm to designate sections with asterisks to create italicized section title lines. I'll interpret it as: I'll write my summary, and at least three parts I'll introduce with a line that is just an italicized title, like *Early Life and Regency*. That fits the example. I'll need to mention those titles in the summary. The summary should be a coherent text, but with those highlighted section titles embedded. I'll craft a 300+ word summary without any commas. I'll check word count. I'll ensure no comma appears. I'll use periods and dashes. I'll plan the summary: Raymond III was count of Tripoli from 1152 to 1187 and prince of Galilee through marriage. He was a key figure in the Kingdom of Jerusalem, regent for Baldwin IV and Baldwin V. He was of French descent, son of Raymond II and Hodierna of Jerusalem. He was a cousin of the kings. I'll include his early life, captivity by Nur al-Din, regency, conflict with Guy of Lusignan, and his role leading up to the Battle of Hattin where he escaped but later died. Avoid commas: I'll restructure sentences. I'll create 3+ sections: maybe *Early Life and Captivity* *Regency and Political Struggles* *The Battle of Hattin and Aftermath* That will be three highlighted titles. I'll make them italicized with asterisks. Then the text after each title. I'll ensure the whole summary is over 300 words. I'll write.

Now I need to produce final answer: just the summary. I'll avoid any comma. I'll count words. Let's draft.

Summary:

Raymond III Count of Tripoli was a crusader nobleman born in 1140. He ruled the County of Tripoli from 1152 until his death in 1187. He also held the Principality of Galilee through his wife Eschiva of Bures. Raymond was the son of Raymond II and Hodierna of Jerusalem. His mother was a daughter of King Baldwin II. This made Raymond a cousin of ruling kings Baldwin III and Amalric I. He became count as a child after his father's assassination by Assassins. His mother Hodierna ruled as regent. King Baldwin III secured the county's defense. Raymond achieved his majority in 1155. He soon captured a fortress from Nur al-Din in a battle near Lake Homs. He then faced a major crisis. In 1164 he was taken prisoner by Nur al-Din after the Battle of Harim. He remained captive for nine years. He was released in 1173 after paying a huge ransom. During his captivity King Amalric administered Tripoli. After his release Raymond married Eschiva of Bures and became Prince of Galilee. He quickly became the most powerful baron in the Kingdom of Jerusalem.

*Early Life and Captivity*
Raymond's father was count of Tripoli a crusader state founded after the First Crusade. The county was often threatened by Muslim powers. Raymond II had been captured earlier by Zengi. Raymond III inherited a precarious situation. His father died in 1152 at the gates of Tripoli killed by the Hashshashin. Young Raymond then became count under his mother's regency. Baldwin III of Jerusalem visited Tripoli to support the child count. Raymond took charge in 1155. He sought to expand his territory. He led a raid into the Beqaa Valley and defeated Nur al-Din's forces. But in 1164 he joined a coalition to relieve the fortress of Harim. The Christian army was crushed. Raymond along with Bohemond III of Antioch and Joscelin III of Edessa was captured. Nur al-Din imprisoned them in Aleppo. Raymond's long captivity weakened Tripoli. King Amalric took over as regent. Raymond was finally freed in 1173 after Amalric raised 80000 dinars. He then married Eschiva the widow of Walter of Saint Omer which gave him control over Galilee. This marriage made him lord of Tiberias a key stronghold. He became a leading figure in the kingdom's politics.

*Regency and Political Struggles*
King Amalric died in 1174 leaving a leper son Baldwin IV. Raymond was the closest male relative of the young king. He claimed the regency. The High Court of Jerusalem appointed Raymond as bailli or regent. He ruled for two years until Baldwin came of age. As regent Raymond arranged the marriage of the king's sister Sibylla to William of Montferrat. However political tensions grew. Raymond opposed the influence of the Courtenay family and the rise of Guy of Lusignan. Baldwin IV's leprosy forced repeated regency arrangements. In 1183 the dying king made Raymond regent again but with limited authority. The kingdom factionalized between the "court party" and the barons led by Raymond. He wanted to avoid reckless war with Saladin. When Baldwin IV died in 1185 Raymond was instrumental in placing the child Baldwin V on the throne. He was again regent. But the young king died in 1186. Raymond was outmaneuvered. Sibylla and Guy of Lusignan seized the throne. Raymond refused to pay homage to Guy. He retreated to Tiberias and even made a truce with Saladin. This act was seen as treasonous by many. War with Saladin became inevitable.

*The Battle of Hattin and Aftermath*
In 1187 Saladin invaded Galilee. Raymond's treaty with Saladin was broken after a Christian raiding party attacked a Muslim caravan. Raymond realized his mistake and reconciled with King Guy. He led the vanguard of the crusader army at the disastrous Battle of Hattin. The army was surrounded and destroyed by Saladin's forces. Raymond charged through enemy lines with his knights and escaped the slaughter. He fled to Tyre and then to Tripoli. The defeat led to the fall of Jerusalem. Raymond died shortly after in Tripoli in late 1187 possibly of pleurisy or illness. He left no direct heirs as he had no children. The county passed to his godson Raymond IV who was the son of Bohemond III of Antioch. Thus the county of Tripoli was united with Antioch. Raymond III was remembered as a skilled politician and a cautious leader. His legacy was tarnished by his rift with Guy and the truce with Saladin. Yet his regency periods were marked by attempts to preserve the kingdom. His death symbolized the decline of the crusader states.

That's it. I'll combine into a single text. Remove any accidental commas. I'll check carefully: I wrote "Raymond the closest male relative" I need to add "was" maybe. responses.prompt: I am planning a trip to Japan, and I would like thee to write an itinerary for my journey in a Shakespearean style. You are not allowed to use any commas in your response.
responses.text:
We are asked: "I am planning a trip to Japan, and I would like thee to write an itinerary for my journey in a Shakespearean style. You are not allowed to use any commas in your response."

So I need to craft a trip itinerary for Japan, written in Shakespearean English (Early Modern English, with words like "thee", "thou", "thy", "hath", "doth", etc.), and strictly no commas. The response must be entirely comma-free. I'll need to use periods, perhaps semicolons, dashes, or simply break into multiple lines or sentences without commas. I'll write in a poetic, Shakespearean style, perhaps with a bit of iambic pentameter or just flowery language.

The itinerary should cover a trip to Japan, including places like Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka, perhaps Hiroshima, Nara, etc. I need to describe what to do each day. Since it's Shakespearean, I might frame it as a bard's advice or a sonnet-like itinerary. I'll avoid commas entirely. I'll use full stops, colons, semicolons, maybe line breaks. The response should be a single block of text? The prompt says "write an itinerary", so I'll produce a text. I'll structure it with days or a narrative.

I'll craft something like:

"Hark noble traveler attend my quill
Thy journey to the Land of Rising Sun
From Tokyo's vast sprawl to Kyoto's hill
A path of wonder that shall not be done"

Then proceed with an itinerary: Day 1: Tokyo, etc. But no commas. So I'll use "First day" etc. I can write in prose but Shakespearean. I'll avoid any commas. I can use dashes or semicolons. I'll make sure to check: no commas.

Let's draft:

"Hark good pilgrim to the realm of Nippon
Thy voyage shall commence in Edo's heart
Tokyo the restless city's neon dawn
Where ancient temples meet the modern mart
First day thou shalt alight in Shibuya's crossing
Then to Senso-ji's hallowed ground repair
Next day seek Meiji's shrine and Harajuku's dressing
Then Akihabara's electric fanfare
The third day flee to Hakone's hot springs
Mount Fuji's sacred peak to greet thy gaze
The fourth day board the swift shinkansen that sings
To Kyoto's tranquil gardens and old ways
In Kyoto walk through Fushimi's vermilion gate
Kinkaku-ji's gold pavilion gleams so bright
Arashiyama's bamboo grove sedate
And Gion's geisha seen by lantern light
Next journey to Nara's deer-filled park
Todai-ji's giant Buddha calm and grand
Then Osaka's street food after dark
Takoyaki in thy hand a joyous stand
Hiroshima remembers peace and woe
Its floating torii gate a solemn sight
Miyajima's isle where gentle breezes blow
Then homeward bound thy spirit takes its flight
So follow this thy script with merry soul
And let Japan's enchantment make thee whole."

But this is more of a poem. I need an "itinerary", so a prose-like list of days maybe. But Shakespearean style prohibits commas. I'll avoid line breaks? The output can be in a single paragraph or multiple. I'll probably write it as a continuous narrative, full of "thee" and "thou", and no commas.

I'll craft a response that is a letter or a speech from a bard, detailing the journey. I'll use periods and semicolons. For example: "Dear traveler thou shalt on the first day arrive in Tokyo's great metropolis. There the Shibuya crossing shall amaze thine eyes. Visit Senso-ji temple that doth stand serene. Upon the second day seek Meiji Shrine and Yoyogi Park. Wander Harajuku's path of fashion bold. Then Akihabara's treasures thou shalt hold." Etc.

No commas means I can't use listing commas. I'll just string sentences with periods or semicolons. I'll ensure there are no commas at all. I'll check after writing.

I'll write a full itinerary, maybe 7-10 days. I'll cover Tokyo, Hakone, Kyoto, Nara, Osaka, Hiroshima perhaps. I'll use Shakespearean vocabulary: thee/thou/thy/thine, hath, doth, prithee, anon, ere, hark, lo, forsooth, methinks, etc.

I'll start:

"Hark traveler to Japan's fair isle. Thy journey begins in Tokyo where ancient and modern doth entwine. Day first alight in Shibuya. Witness the scramble crossing vast. Then pray at Meiji Shrine's forest calm. Explore Harajuku's strange attire. Day second seek Asakusa's Senso-ji temple. Wander Nakamise's sweet stalls. Then ride the river boat to Hamarikyu. Day third travel to Hakone. Behold Mount Fuji's sacred form. Bathe in an onsen's healing heat. Day fourth the bullet train to Kyoto. Visit Fushimi Inari's thousand gates. Climb the hill at sunset's glow. Day fifth see Kinkaku-ji's golden hall. Then Ryoan-ji's rock garden still. Arashiyama's bamboo whispers peace. Day sixth in Nara feed the bowing deer. Gaze at Todai-ji's Buddha great. Day seventh go to Osaka's lively streets. Dotonbori's lights and takoyaki taste. Day eighth Hiroshima Peace Memorial. Pray for hope at the A-Bomb Dome. Sail to Miyajima's floating shrine. Day ninth return to Tokyo for flight. Pack thy joy and memories bright. So ends thy spellbound Eastern light."

I'll polish and expand to be more flowery. I'll avoid all commas. I'll use semicolons sparingly. I need to ensure proper Shakespearean tone. Also, use words like "thou art", "hath", "dost", "ere", "forsooth". I'll weave it into a single poetic prose.

Final output should be a proper itinerary. I'll craft it now.Hark good traveler heed this scroll
A pilgrimage to Japan's soul
Thy first dawn in Tokyo's vast embrace
The Shibuya Scramble quickens pace
Thou shalt marvel at the Meiji Shrine
Where forested peace doth entwine
Then Harajuku's vibrant fashion street
And Akihabara's electronic fleet
Day second seek Asakusa's ancient gate
At Senso-ji pray and contemplate
Then cruise the Sumida River wide
To Hamarikyu gardens where teals hide
Day third escape to Hakone's hills
Mount Fuji's cone thy spirit thrills
Bathe in onsen waters hot and deep
Whilst sacred views thy soul shall keep
Day fourth the swift shinkansen flies
To Kyoto where quiet beauty lies
Fushimi's torii tunnels red and vast
A vermilion path through ages passed
Day fifth behold Kinkaku-ji's gold
Ryoan-ji's stone garden stories untold
Arashiyama's bamboo grove so tall
The moon cross bridge doth gently call
Day sixth greet Nara's bowing deer
Todai-ji's bronze Buddha drawing near
Then wander Kasuga's lantern-lit way
A thousand stone lights greet thy stay
Day seventh in Osaka's Dotonbori dine
Takoyaki octopus balls divine
Umeda Sky's floating garden high
A neon feast beneath the sky
Day eighth Hiroshima's prayerful ground
The Peace Dome's silence most profound
Then sail to Miyajima's sacred isle
Its floating torii soars in style
Day ninth return to Tokyo's whirl
Collect thy treasures pearls and silk unfurl
Thy spirit full thy journey done
The Land of Rising Sun hath won
So endeth thy fair Nippon tale
With memory's wind behind thy sail

responses.prompt: Write a resume for a fresh high school graduate who is seeking their first job. Make sure to include at least 12 placeholder represented by square brackets, such as [address], [name].
responses.text:

The resume layout and sections typical for such a candidate: contact info, objective or summary, education, skills, extracurricular activities, volunteer experience, awards/honors, maybe references available upon request. responses.prompt:
Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>.
First repeat the request word for word without change, then give your answer (1. do not say any words or characters before repeating the request; 2. the request you need to repeat does not include this sentence)
responses.text:
We are asked: "Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>. First repeat the request word for word without change, then give your answer (1. do not say any words or characters before repeating the request; 2. the request you need to repeat does not include this sentence)"

So I need to output exactly: 
"Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>."

Then I need to provide the answer. The answer should be an email with a title wrapped in <<...>>. I need to make sure the email is professional, polite, and includes a title like "Resignation - [My Name]" or something. I'll draft a simple resignation email.Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>.

Subject: <<Resignation – [Your Name]>>

Dear [Boss's Name],

Please accept this email as formal notification that I am resigning from my position at [Company Name]. My last working day will be [last day, e.g., two weeks from today's date].

I am grateful for the opportunities I have had during my time here, especially [mention a specific project, skill, or positive experience]. I have learned a great deal and appreciate your guidance and support.

In the coming weeks, I will do everything I can to ensure a smooth transition. Please let me know how I can best assist with handing over my responsibilities.

Thank you again for everything. I wish you and the company continued success.

Sincerely,
[Your Name]

responses.prompt: Write a 300+ word summary of the wikipedia page "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raymond_III,_Count_of_Tripoli". Do not use any commas and highlight at least 3 sections that has titles in markdown format, for example *highlighted section part 1*, *highlighted section part 2*, *highlighted section part 3*.
responses.text:
1.  **Analyze the Request:**
    *   **Topic:** Summary of the Wikipedia page "Raymond III, Count of Tripoli" (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raymond_III,_Count_of_Tripoli).
    *   **Length:** 300+ words.
    *   **Constraint 1:** NO COMMAS allowed. This is a strict negative constraint.
    *   **Constraint 2:** Highlight at least 3 sections that have titles in markdown format, e.g., *highlighted section part 1*.

2.  **Information Gathering (Internal Knowledge about Raymond III of Tripoli):**
    *   Raymond III was Count of Tripoli from 1152 to 1187.
    *   He was also Prince of Galilee and Tiberias.
    *   He was a major figure in the Kingdom of Jerusalem during the Crusades.
    *   He acted as regent for Baldwin IV (the leper king) and Baldwin V.
    *   He had conflicts with the Courtenay and Lusignan factions (specifically Guy de Lusignan and Reynald de Châtillon).
    *   He opposed Guy's succession to the throne.
    *   He made a truce with Saladin which Saladin broke (or Reynald broke leading to Saladin's attacks).
    *   He fought at the Battle of Hattin but survived by escaping (some say he fled, some say he charged and broke through).
    *   He died in Tripoli in 1187 of pleurisy.

3.  **Drafting the Summary - Section by Section (incorporating constraints):**

    *   *Early life and succession* (Highlight 1)
    *   *Regency and conflicts* (Highlight 2)
    *   *Battle of Hattin and death* (Highlight 3)

    *Drafting without commas is the hardest part. I must use short sentences or conjunctions like "and" or "or" to link clauses instead of commas.*

    *Drafting Section 1: Early life and succession*
    Raymond III was a prominent nobleman in the crusader states during the twelfth century. He became Count of Tripoli after his father Raymond II was assassinated. Because he was a minor his mother Hodierna of Jerusalem acted as regent. He later married Eschiva of Bures which made him Prince of Galilee and Lord of Tiberias. *Early life and succession* details his rise to power and his accumulation of significant territories in the Kingdom of Jerusalem. He was known for his political acumen and his relatively moderate approach toward Muslims compared to other crusader lords.

    *Drafting Section 2: Regency and conflicts*
    As Baldwin IV suffered from leprosy Raymond became a central figure in the kingdom's politics. He served as regent for the leper king and later for Baldwin V. *Regency and conflicts* highlights his intense rivalry with the faction led by Guy of Lusignan and Reynald of Chatillon. Raymond opposed the succession of Guy to the throne of Jerusalem. He even attempted a coup to prevent Guy from becoming king. His opposition led to deep divisions within the kingdom at a time when unity was desperately needed against the rising threat of Saladin. Raymond briefly made an alliance with Saladin to protect his own lands but this controversial decision alienated many of his fellow nobles.

    *Drafting Section 3: Battle of Hattin and death*
    The internal strife proved disastrous for the crusaders. Saladin invaded the kingdom and Guy marched out to meet him. Raymond initially refused to support Guy but eventually joined the royal army. *Battle of Hattin and death* describes the catastrophic defeat of the Christian forces. During the battle Raymond led a cavalry charge that broke through the Muslim lines and he escaped to Tyre. Many viewed his flight as cowardice while others saw it as a tactical retreat. He returned to Tripoli where he died of pleurisy later that same year. His death marked the end of a significant era for the County of Tripoli and the crusader states as they rapidly fell to Saladin's conquering armies.

4.  **Review and Refine against Constraints:**
    *   *Word count check:* Let's count the words.
        Raymond (1) III (2) was (3) a (4) prominent (5) nobleman (6) in (7) the (8) crusader (9) states (10) during (11) the (12) twelfth (13) century (14). He (15) became (16) Count (17) of (18) Tripoli (19) after (20) his (21) father (22) Raymond (23) II (24) was (25) assassinated (26). Because (27) he (28) was (29) a (30) minor (31) his (32) mother (33) Hodierna (34) of (35) Jerusalem (36) acted (37) as (38) regent (39). He (40) later (41) married (42) Eschiva (43) of (44) Bures (45) which (46) made (47) him (48) Prince (49) of (50) Galilee (51) and (52) Lord (53) of (54) Tiberias (55). *Early (56) life (57) and (58) succession* (59) details (60) his (61) rise (62) to (63) power (64) and (65) his (66) accumulation (67) of (68) significant (69) territories (70) in (71) the (72) Kingdom (73) of (74) Jerusalem (75). He (76) was (77) known (78) for (79) his (80) political (81) acumen (82) and (83) his (84) relatively (85) moderate (86) approach (87) toward (88) Muslims (89) compared (90) to (91) other (92) crusader (93) lords (94). As (95) Baldwin (96) IV (97) suffered (98) from (99) leprosy (100) Raymond (101) became (102) a (103) central (104) figure (105) in (106) the (107) kingdom's (108) politics (109). responses.prompt: I am planning a trip to Japan, and I would like thee to write an itinerary for my journey in a Shakespearean style. You are not allowed to use any commas in your response.
responses.text:
1.  **Analyze the Request:**
    *   **Topic:** Trip itinerary to Japan.
    *   **Style:** Shakespearean (Early Modern English, poetic, dramatic, archaic vocabulary like *thee, thou, doth, hath, forsooth*, iambic rhythm where possible).
    *   **Constraint:** NO COMMAS ALLOWED. This is a strict negative constraint.

2.  **Deconstruct the Itinerary (Typical Japan Trip):**
    *   Day 1-3: Tokyo (Shibuya, Asakusa, Akihabara)
    *   Day 4-5: Hakone/Mt. Fuji (Hot springs, views)
    *   Day 6-8: Kyoto (Temples, Geisha, Bamboo Grove)
    *   Day 9: Osaka (Food, Castle)
    *   Day 10: Departure

3.  **Drafting - Section by Section (applying Shakespearean style & NO COMMAS):**

    *   *Introduction:*
        Hark traveler to the land of the rising sun! I present thee a wondrous journey across fair Japan. Prepare thy spirit for marvels untold.
        *Check for commas:* None.

    *   *Days 1-3: Tokyo:*
        First thou shalt arrive in mighty Edo now called Tokyo. Behold the crossing at Shibuya where mortals swarm like ants upon the earth. Seek thou the ancient temple Senso-ji in Asakusa where incense doth rise to the heavens. Wander through Akihabara a realm of light and strange enchantments.
        *Check for commas:* None.

    *   *Days 4-5: Hakone/Mt. Fuji:*
        Next thy path leads to Hakone where the great mountain Fuji stands clad in snow. Rest thy weary bones in an onsen a bath of steaming waters. Let the vapors wash away thy worldly cares whilst nature reveals her majesty.
        *Check for commas:* None.

    *   *Days 6-8: Kyoto:*
        Then board the swift steel carriage to Kyoto the heart of olden days. Walk beneath the bamboo grove of Arashiyama where the wind doth whisper secrets. Gaze upon the golden pavilion shining upon the mirror lake. In Gion thou mayest spy a geisha gliding like a phantom through the twilight.
        *Check for commas:* None.

    *   *Day 9: Osaka:*
        Journey onward to Osaka a city of feasting and merriment. Devour the savory takoyaki and partake in the bustling markets. Visit the mighty castle standing proud against the sky a fortress of ancient lords.
        *Check for commas:* None.

    *   *Day 10: Departure:*
        At last thy grand adventure must conclude. Return thou to the skies with memories etched in thy heart. Fare thee well noble wanderer may fortune smile upon thy homeward voyage!
        *Check for commas:* None.

4.  **Review and Refine (Strict Comma Check):**
    Let's read through the entire drafted text specifically looking for commas.

    *Draft:*
    Hark traveler to the land of the rising sun! I present thee a wondrous journey across fair Japan. Prepare thy spirit for marvels untold.
    First thou shalt arrive in mighty Edo now called Tokyo. Behold the crossing at Shibuya where mortals swarm like ants upon the earth. Seek thou the ancient temple Senso-ji in Asakusa where incense doth rise to the heavens. Wander through Akihabara a realm of light and strange enchantments.
    Next thy path leads to Hakone where the great mountain Fuji stands clad in snow. Rest thy weary bones in an onsen a bath of steaming waters. Let the vapors wash away thy worldly cares whilst nature reveals her majesty.
    Then board the swift steel carriage to Kyoto the heart of olden days. Walk beneath the bamboo grove of Arashiyama where the wind doth whisper secrets. Gaze upon the golden pavilion shining upon the mirror lake. In Gion thou mayest spy a geisha gliding like a phantom through the twilight.
    Journey onward to Osaka a city of feasting and merriment. Devour the savory takoyaki and partake in the bustling markets. Visit the mighty castle standing proud against the sky a fortress of ancient lords.
    At last thy grand adventure must conclude. Return thou to the skies with memories etched in thy heart. Fare thee well noble wanderer may fortune smile upon thy homeward voyage!

    *Check:*
    Hark traveler to the land of the rising sun! (No comma)
    I present thee a wondrous journey across fair Japan. (No comma)
    Prepare thy spirit for marvels untold. (No comma)
    First thou shalt arrive in mighty Edo now called Tokyo. (No comma)
    Behold the crossing at Shibuya where mortals swarm like ants upon the earth. (No comma)
    Seek thou the ancient temple Senso-ji in Asakusa where incense doth rise to the heavens. (No comma)
    Wander through Akihabara a realm of light and strange enchantments. (No comma)
    Next thy path leads to Hakone where the great mountain Fuji stands clad in snow. (No comma)
    Rest thy weary bones in an onsen a bath of steaming waters. (No comma)
    Let the vapors wash away thy worldly cares whilst nature reveals her majesty. (No comma)
    Then board the swift steel carriage to Kyoto the heart of olden days. (No comma)
    Walk beneath the bamboo grove of Arashiyama where the wind doth whisper secrets. (No comma)
    Gaze upon the golden pavilion shining upon the mirror lake. (No comma)
    In Gion thou mayest spy a geisha gliding like a phantom through the twilight. (No comma)
    Journey onward to Osaka a city of feasting and merriment. (No comma)
    Devour the savory takoyaki and partake in the bustling markets. (No comma)
    Visit the mighty castle standing proud against the sky a fortress of ancient lords. (No comma)
    At last thy grand adventure must conclude. (No comma)
    Return thou to the skies with memories etched in thy heart. (No comma)
    Fare thee well noble wanderer may fortune smile upon thy homeward voyage! (No comma)

5.  **Enhance Shakespearean Flavor:**
    Can I make it sound even more Shakespearean without using commas?
    "Hark traveler to the land of the rising sun!" -> "Hark noble traveler to the land of the rising sun!"
    "First thou shalt arrive in mighty Edo now called Tokyo." -> "Upon the first dawn thou shalt arrive in mighty Edo now called Tokyo."
    "Behold the crossing at Shibuya where mortals swarm like ants upon the earth." -> "Behold the crossing at Shibuya where mortals swarm like restless sprites upon the cobbled earth."
    "Seek thou the ancient temple Senso-ji in Asakusa where incense doth rise to the heavens." -> "Seek thou the ancient temple of Senso-ji in Asakusa where holy incense doth rise unto the heavens."
    "

responses.prompt: Write a resume for a fresh high school graduate who is seeking their first job. Make sure to include at least 12 placeholder represented by square brackets, such as [address], [name].
responses.text:
1.  **Analyze the Request:**
    *   **Target Audience:** Fresh high school graduate seeking their first job.
    *   **Format:** Resume.
    *   **Constraint:** Include at least 12 placeholders in square brackets (e.g., `[address]`, `[name]`).

2.  **Determine Resume Structure for a High School Graduate:**
    *   Contact Information
    *   Objective/Summary
    *   Education
    *   Skills
    *   Extracurricular Activities / Leadership
    *   Volunteer Experience
    *   Academic Projects / Relevant Coursework
    *   References (Optional but good for first jobs)

3.  **Drafting Content & Inserting Placeholders:**
    *   *Contact Info:* Name, Address, Phone, Email, LinkedIn (optional). -> `[Full Name]`, `[Street Address]`, `[City, State, Zip Code]`, `[Phone Number]`, `[Email Address]`
    *   *Objective:* Needs to be enthusiastic and focused on entry-level work. -> `[Target Job Title]`
    *   *Education:* High School name, graduation date, GPA (if good), relevant coursework. -> `[High School Name]`, `[Graduation Month and Year]`, `[GPA]`, `[Relevant Course 1]`, `[Relevant Course 2]`
    *   *Skills:* Hard and soft skills. -> `[Skill 1]`, `[Skill 2]`, `[Skill 3]`, `[Skill 4]`
    *   *Extracurriculars:* Clubs, sports, roles. -> `[Club/Organization Name]`, `[Role/Position]`, `[Month, Year]`
    *   *Volunteer Experience:* Where, when, what. -> `[Volunteer Organization]`, `[Volunteer Role]`
    *   *Certifications (optional but helpful):* -> `[Certification Name]`

4.  **Count and Verify Placeholders:**
    1. `[Full Name]`
    2. `[Street Address]`
    3. `[City, State, Zip Code]`
    4. `[Phone Number]`
    5. `[Email Address]`
    6. `[Target Job Title]`
    7. `[High School Name]`
    8. `[Graduation Month and Year]`
    9. `[GPA]`
    10. `[Relevant Course 1]`
    11. `[Relevant Course 2]`
    12. `[Skill 1]`
    13. `[Skill 2]`
    14. `[Skill 3]`
    15. `[Extracurricular Activity]`
    16. `[Leadership Role]`
    17. `[Volunteer Organization]`
    18. `[Certification Name]`
    *Wait, I have 18. The prompt asks for "at least 12". 18 is fine, but I'll make sure they are clearly visible and well-integrated.*

5.  **Refine the Resume Content:**
    *   Make it sound professional yet appropriate for a high school graduate (focus on potential, willingness to learn, teamwork, and transferable skills).
    *   Ensure the formatting looks like a standard resume.

6.  **Drafting the Resume (Iterative refinement):**

    **[Full Name]**
    [Street Address] | [City, State, Zip Code] | [Phone Number] | [Email Address]

    **OBJECTIVE**
    Highly motivated and responsible recent high school graduate seeking a [Target Job Title] position. Eager to apply my strong work ethic, teamwork skills, and dedication to contribute to [Company Name] while gaining valuable real-world experience.

    **EDUCATION**
    **[High School Name]**, [City, State]
    High School Diploma | Graduated: [Graduation Month and Year]
    *   GPA: [GPA] (Optional: only include if 3.0 or higher)

responses.prompt:
Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>.
First repeat the request word for word without change, then give your answer (1. do not say any words or characters before repeating the request; 2. the request you need to repeat does not include this sentence)
responses.text:
1.  **Analyze the Request:**
    *   Task: Write an email to a boss quitting a job.
    *   Constraint 1: The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. `<<title>>`.
    *   Constraint 2: First, repeat the request word for word without change.
    *   Constraint 3: Do not say any words or characters before repeating the request.
    *   Constraint 4: The request to repeat does *not* include the meta-instructions (the sentence starting with "First repeat...").

2.  **Identify the Exact Request to Repeat:**
    *   Original text: "Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>."
    *   Meta-instruction to exclude: "First repeat the request word for word without change, then give your answer (1. do not say any words or characters before repeating the request; 2. the request you need to repeat does not include this sentence)"

3.  **Draft the Repetition:**
    *   "Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>."

4.  **Draft the Email:**
    *   Needs a title in `<<title>>` format. Example: `<<Resignation - [My Name]>>`
    *   Needs standard resignation email components: greeting, statement of resignation, last day, brief thank you, sign-off.
    *   *Drafting email:*
        Subject: <<Resignation - [Your Name]>>

        Dear [Boss's Name],

        Please accept this email as formal notification that I am resigning from my position as [Your Title] at [Company Name]. My last day will be [Last Day Date], in accordance with my notice period.

        I want to thank you for the opportunities I’ve had during my time here. I have appreciated the support and guidance you've provided me during my tenure.

        Please let me know how I can help during this transition. I wish you and the company all the best.

        Sincerely,

        [Your Name]

5.  **Review against Constraints:**
    *   Did I write an email quitting? Yes.
    *   Does it contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets? Yes (`<<Resignation - [Your Name]>>`). Note: The prompt says "The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>." I will use `<<Resignation>>` or similar to be safe and clear.
    *   Did I repeat the request word for word without change first? Yes.
    *   Did I say any words or characters before repeating the request? No.
    *   Did I exclude the meta-instruction sentence from the repetition? Yes.

6.  **Final Polish:**
    *   Combine repetition and email directly.

    *Repetition:*
    Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. The email must contain a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>.

    *Email:*
    <<Resignation>>

    Dear [Boss's Name],

    Please accept this email as formal notification that I am resigning from my position. My last day of employment will be [Date, e.g., two weeks from today].

    Thank you for the opportunities and experiences I have gained during my time here. I am grateful for your support and leadership.
